lessons: Create strong passwords and keep them private
Spot online scams and fake offers
Protect your phone with lock screen and updates
Share safely on WhatsApp and social media
What the internet is and how it works
Use search engines to find trusted information
Open websites safely and avoid harmful links
Manage mobile data and browse efficiently

step1.heading: What is a smartphone and what makes it smart?
step1.content: A smartphone is a small but powerful computer that fits inside your hand. It can make calls and send messages, but it can also do many other things like browsing the internet, taking pictures, and playing music. What makes it “smart” is that it can run different apps, which are small programs that help you do specific tasks. It is like carrying a camera, radio, calculator, and telephone all inside one small device. If you treat it well and learn step by step, you will be surprised how much it can help your daily life.

step2.heading: The outside of your phone (the parts you can touch)
step2.content: Let’s look at the physical parts of a smartphone so you know what is what. The screen is the glass front where you see everything and touch to control the phone. The power button is used to turn the phone on or off, and it also helps you lock and unlock the screen. The volume buttons increase or reduce sound, like when you are listening to music or receiving a call. The charging port is where you plug your charger to give the phone power. The front camera is for selfies and video calls, while the back camera is usually for clearer photos, and the speaker is where you hear sound when you play audio or put a call on loudspeaker.

step3.heading: The home screen and what is on it
step3.content: The home screen is the first main page you see after you unlock your phone. It is like the “front room” of your phone where you keep the things you use often. The small pictures you see are app icons, and each icon opens an app when you tap it. At the top, you will see the status bar, which shows important information like the time, battery level, and network signal. At the bottom, many phones have a dock, which is a fixed row of your most-used apps like Phone, Messages, or Chrome. Once you understand the home screen, using your phone becomes much easier because you always know where to start.

step4.heading: How to navigate your phone using gestures
step4.content: Your phone responds to your fingers, so learning simple gestures makes everything easier. Tapping means touching once to open something, like opening an app icon. Swiping means dragging your finger across the screen, like swiping left or right to change pages or swiping up to go home on many phones. Swiping down from the top opens notifications, where you can see messages and quick buttons like WiFi or torch. Holding (long-press) means pressing and holding on an icon to see more options, like moving an app or deleting it. Pinching with two fingers helps you zoom in or zoom out on photos and some websites, like bringing a paper closer to your eyes to see it better.

step5.heading: Understanding your phone settings
step5.content: Settings is like the control room of your phone, where you can change how everything works. If your phone is too bright, too quiet, or not connecting to WiFi, settings is where you fix it. To find settings, look for an icon that looks like a small gear, usually called Settings. Tap it and you will see different sections like Display, Sound, Network, Battery, and Storage. Do not be afraid of settings—just move slowly and read what you see. If you are unsure, you can always press back; settings will not spoil your phone just because you opened it.

step6.heading: Taking care of your phone
step6.content: A phone is like an important personal tool, so taking care of it saves you money and stress. Clean the screen gently with a soft cloth so it stays clear and doesn’t scratch. Use a screen protector and a case, because one small fall can break the screen like a glass cup. Try not to expose the phone to water, even small rain, because water can damage the inside parts. Avoid extreme heat, like leaving it under the sun in a car or on a hot window, because heat can spoil the battery. If you take care of your phone, it will last longer and work better for you every day.

step1.heading: What is the phone app and where to find it?
step1.content: Every smartphone has a built-in phone app for making calls. It often looks like a green telephone icon, and many phones keep it on the home screen or the dock at the bottom. The phone app is like your “call room” where you dial numbers and speak to people. Even if you install many apps, this phone app is always there because calls are a basic phone function. If you can’t find it quickly, you can swipe up to see all apps and search for “Phone.” Once you locate it, keep it in an easy place so you don’t struggle when you need to call someone.

step2.heading: How to make a call
step2.content: To make a call, open the phone app and look for the keypad, which is the number buttons like on a small calculator. Tap the numbers carefully to type the phone number you want to call. When you are done, press the green call button and the phone will start calling that number. If you hear ringing, it means your call is going through and the other person’s phone is ringing too. When the person answers, you will hear their voice and you can talk normally. If the call does not connect, it may be network issue, wrong number, or low airtime.

step3.heading: How to receive a call
step3.content: When someone calls you, your screen will light up and you will hear a ringtone. You may see the caller’s name if you saved the person as a contact, or you may see only the number. Many phones will show an answer button you tap, or you may need to swipe to answer depending on your phone type. To decline a call, you can tap decline or swipe away, especially if you are busy or in class. Declining is not rude—sometimes you can call back later when you are free. You can also silence the ringing by pressing the volume button, then decide whether to answer or not.

step4.heading: How to save a contact
step4.content: Saving a contact means keeping a phone number with a name so you don’t have to memorize it. After a call, you may see an option like “Add to contacts,” or you can open the Contacts app directly. Tap add new contact, then type the person’s name and phone number. You can also add other details like a second number or a photo, but name and number is enough to start. When you press save, the contact stays on your phone and you can find it easily next time. This is very useful for family, close friends, and important numbers.

step5.heading: How to check your call history
step5.content: Call history, also called call log, shows you the list of calls you made, received, or missed. In the phone app, look for Recents or Call history and tap it. You will see names or numbers with times showing when the call happened. Missed calls are often marked with a red sign or a different color, so you notice them quickly. If you want to call someone back, you can tap their name or number from the call log and press call. This is helpful when you missed a call and want to return it without asking for the number again.

step6.heading: Phone call tips for everyday Nigerian life
step6.content: Before making a long call, it is smart to check your airtime balance so the call does not cut suddenly. If you don’t have airtime, you can use “flash me” or “please call me” to ask someone to call you back, depending on your network. Save important numbers like your parents, close relatives, your school, and emergency contacts so you don’t panic in an urgent moment. If there is noise around you, move to a quieter place so the other person can hear you clearly. Also be careful when someone calls you claiming they are from a bank or network—don’t share secret information like OTP codes. A calm and smart caller avoids stress and stays safe.

step1.heading: What is a text message and why use it?
step1.content: A text message, also called SMS, is a short written message sent from one phone to another. It is useful when you cannot talk on a call, like when you are in class or in a noisy place. It is also helpful when your airtime is low, because SMS can be cheaper than a long call. A message can carry information that the person can read later, like an address or meeting time. SMS works using your network signal, so you don’t need internet for basic text messages. Knowing SMS helps you communicate even when data is off.

step2.heading: How to open the messages app
step2.content: Most phones have a built-in messages app that looks like a speech bubble icon. Look for it on your home screen or in the list of apps when you swipe up. Tap the messages icon and you will see your inbox, which is the list of conversations. Each conversation is like a folder that contains the messages between you and one person. If this is your first time, it may look empty and that is normal. Once you open it, you are ready to read messages or send a new one.

step3.heading: How to write and send a new message
step3.content: To send a new message, tap the compose button, which may look like a pencil, plus sign, or “new message.” Next, type the person’s number or choose their name from your contacts. Then tap inside the text box and type your message clearly. When you are ready, tap the send button, which may look like an arrow or “send.” After you send it, your message will appear in the conversation so you can see what you wrote. If it fails to send, check your network signal and make sure you typed the correct number.

step4.heading: How to read and reply to a message
step4.content: When someone sends you a message, it will appear in your inbox with their name or number. Tap the conversation to open it and read the messages inside. A conversation is also called a thread, which simply means the full chat history with that person in one place. To reply, tap the text box, type your response, and tap send. Your reply will appear under the previous messages, so you can follow the discussion easily. This makes messaging feel like a simple back-and-forth conversation.

step5.heading: Understanding message status icons
step5.content: Different apps show different status icons to tell you what is happening with your message. On WhatsApp, one tick usually means the message was sent, two ticks mean it reached the person’s phone, and blue ticks mean the person opened and read it. On regular SMS, you may not always see ticks, because SMS works differently and depends on your network. WhatsApp and other internet messaging apps need data or WiFi, while SMS uses your network signal and airtime. Knowing this difference helps you understand why WhatsApp may not work when you don’t have data, but SMS can still send. If you see a status you don’t understand, don’t worry—focus on whether your message is going through.

step6.heading: Messaging tips and etiquette
step6.content: Be respectful in messages, because written words can easily sound rude when the other person cannot hear your tone. Avoid sharing personal information like passwords, bank details, or OTP codes over messages, even if someone begs you. Be careful with unknown numbers that ask for money or claim you won something—many are scams. If a message looks suspicious, it is better to ignore it and ask a trusted person for advice. Save important messages like addresses or school details so you can find them later. Good messaging habits keep you safe and make people trust you more.

step1.heading: What is an app and what can apps do?
step1.content: An app is a program you install on your phone to do a specific job. For example, WhatsApp helps you send messages, YouTube helps you watch videos, and Opay or Kuda can help you send money. Apps are like different shops in a market—each shop has its own product. Some apps help you learn, some help you communicate, and some help you manage daily life. The good thing is that you can choose the apps you need and remove the ones you don’t use. Once you understand apps, your phone becomes more useful and personal to you.

step2.heading: What is the Google Play Store?
step2.content: The Google Play Store is the official shop for Android apps. Think of it like a well-organized market where you can find many things, and most of them are free. Because it is official, it is usually the safest place to download apps compared to random websites. In the Play Store, you can search for apps, read what they do, and see reviews from other people. You can also update your apps from there, which helps keep your phone secure. If you are not sure where to get an app, start with the Play Store.

step3.heading: How to search for and install an app
step3.content: Open the Play Store and tap the search bar at the top. Type the name of the app you want, like WhatsApp, then press search. Tap the app that looks correct, and read the name carefully so you don’t install a fake one. You can check the description and some reviews to see if people are happy with it. When you are ready, tap Install and wait for it to finish downloading and installing. After it installs, you can tap Open or find the app icon on your home screen.

step4.heading: How to update and uninstall apps
step4.content: Updating apps is important because updates often fix problems and improve security. In the Play Store, you can check for updates in the menu under Manage apps & device. If you see Update available, tap update and wait for it to finish. Uninstalling means removing an app you no longer need, which helps free storage space and can make your phone faster. You can uninstall from the Play Store or by holding the app icon and choosing uninstall. Don’t be afraid to remove apps you don’t use; you can always install them again later. Keeping apps clean and updated is like keeping your room tidy—it reduces stress.

step5.heading: Understanding app permissions
step5.content: When you install an app, it may ask for permission to access things like your camera, contacts, microphone, or location. Permissions are like asking for access to a room in your house—some requests make sense, some don’t. For example, a camera app needs camera permission, and WhatsApp may need contacts to show names. But if a simple calculator app asks for your contacts or location, that is suspicious and you should be careful. You can deny permissions you don’t want, and many apps will still work. You can also manage permissions later in Settings under Apps or Privacy.

step6.heading: Staying safe when downloading apps
step6.content: Be careful because some fake apps look like real ones but are made to steal your information. Always download apps from the Play Store, not from random websites or links sent on WhatsApp. Before installing, check the app name, the developer, the number of downloads, and reviews. If an app has very few downloads or many angry reviews, avoid it. Also be careful when an app asks for too many permissions that don’t match what it does. Staying safe with apps protects your money, your data, and your peace of mind.

step1.heading: Understanding your phone battery
step1.content: The battery is what powers your phone, and you can see it as a percentage at the top of the screen. When your battery is 100%, it means your phone is fully charged and can work for many hours. When it is around 50%, it is half, and you should start thinking about charging if you will be outside for long. When it reaches 10% or lower, your phone may start warning you, and it can switch off anytime. Battery is like a small fuel tank—when it is low, the phone cannot keep running. Learning to watch your battery helps you avoid sudden shutdown in important moments.

step2.heading: How to charge your phone correctly
step2.content: Use the correct charger that matches your phone, because a wrong or fake charger can spoil your battery. Plug the charger gently into the charging port and make sure it is firm, not shaking. When charging starts, you should see a charging sign on your screen or the battery icon will change. When it reaches 100%, you can unplug, and it is good not to leave it plugged in all night every night. Charging safety is important, so avoid charging on wet surfaces or near water. If your charger becomes very hot, stop using it and get a safer one.

step3.heading: How to make your battery last longer
step3.content: Reduce screen brightness, because bright light uses more battery like a big bulb in the house. Turn off WiFi and mobile data when you are not using them, because they keep searching for network in the background. Close apps you are not using, especially heavy apps like video apps, because they can drain battery quietly. Many phones have Battery Saver mode, which limits background activity and helps the phone last longer. Also, avoid playing too many games or watching long videos when you know you can’t charge soon. Small habits can make a big difference in battery life.

step4.heading: What is phone storage and why it matters
step4.content: Storage is the space inside your phone where your photos, videos, apps, and files are kept. Think of storage like a bag that can only carry a certain amount; once it is full, you can’t add more easily. When storage is full, your phone may become slow, and apps can start misbehaving. You may also find it difficult to take new photos, download files, or update apps. Storage problems can make your phone frustrating even if the battery is fine. Understanding storage helps you keep your phone smooth and reliable.

step5.heading: How to check and free up storage space
step5.content: To check storage, go to Settings and look for Storage or Device care. You will see what is using space, like photos, videos, apps, and downloads. Start by deleting old videos, duplicate photos, and files you don’t need, especially large ones. You can also uninstall apps you don’t use, because apps can take a lot of space quietly. After cleaning, restart your phone so it can run fresh. Doing this regularly is like cleaning your room—things become easier to find and your phone works faster.

step6.heading: Using external storage and cloud backup
step6.content: A memory card is a small card you can put in some phones to give extra storage space. It is like adding another bag to carry more things, especially photos and videos. If your phone supports it, you can move files to the memory card to free up internal space. Cloud backup means saving your files on the internet so they are safe even if your phone is lost or damaged. For example, Google Photos can help you back up your pictures when you have internet. Think of it like keeping a spare copy of your important photos in a safe place outside your room. This habit protects your memories and reduces stress.

step1.heading: What is the internet, and where did it come from?
step1.content: The internet is like a giant invisible network that connects millions of phones, computers, and other devices all over the world. Think of it like a very big telephone network, but instead of only carrying calls, it carries information like messages, videos, photos, and news. Years ago, people wanted a way for computers in different places to share information quickly, even if they were far apart. Over time, that idea grew into what we now call the internet. Today, when you use the internet, you are connecting to that big network to receive and send information.

step2.heading: How does the internet reach your phone?
step2.content: The internet can reach your phone mainly in two ways: mobile data and WiFi. Mobile data is like buying credit (airtime) for calls, but this credit is for internet use—once it finishes, your internet stops until you buy again. WiFi is like a tap of water that is available when you are close to it, like at home, school, a café, or someone’s shop. When you connect to WiFi, your phone is using that nearby connection to reach the internet. When you are not close to WiFi, mobile data becomes your option. Both are just different ways of connecting your phone to the same internet outside.

step3.heading: What can you actually do on the internet?
step3.content: With the internet, you can do many useful things that can help your daily life and your future. You can chat with friends and family on WhatsApp, send pictures, and even do voice notes when typing is hard. You can watch videos on YouTube to learn skills like cooking, makeup, football highlights, or even how to fix a phone problem. You can search for school information, like answers to a topic, definitions, or past questions, but you should always confirm from more than one source. You can also send money or pay for things using bank apps or transfer platforms, which can save you stress of moving around. You can read news, follow sports, and learn about opportunities like scholarships and jobs.

step4.heading: The internet is not inside your phone
step4.content: Many people think the internet lives inside their phone, but that is not true. Your phone is just a tool that helps you connect to the internet, the same way a radio helps you catch a radio signal. The music is not inside the radio; the radio is only receiving the signal from outside. In the same way, the internet is outside your phone, and your phone connects to it using mobile data or WiFi. That is why sometimes your phone can be fine, but the internet is not working—maybe there is no network, or your data is finished. Understanding this helps you solve problems calmly when your internet is slow or not connecting.

step5.heading: Is the internet safe?
step5.content: The internet is powerful and useful, but you must use it with sense. Think of the internet like a very busy market: there are honest traders selling good things, and there are also people trying to cheat others. You will find helpful teachers, good news, and real opportunities online, but you can also find fake messages and scams. The good thing is that you can stay safe by learning simple rules like not sharing passwords and not clicking suspicious links. You do not need to be scared; you just need to be careful and smart. In this course, you will learn the safe way to use the internet and enjoy the good side of it.

step6.heading: You are now connected to the world
step6.content: When you understand the internet, it feels like you have opened a door to the world. You can learn from people far away, discover new ideas, and find opportunities you may not see around you every day. Even small steps like searching for a word you don’t understand or watching a short tutorial can make you grow. The more you practice, the more confident you will become, and the easier it will feel. Do not rush—learning the internet is like learning a new road; with time you will know it well. The rest of this course will teach you how to use the internet safely and confidently, step by step.

lesson2.title: Connecting to WiFi and mobile data

step1.heading: What is mobile data and how does it work?
step1.content: Mobile data is the internet you buy from your network provider like MTN, Airtel, Glo, or 9mobile. It is similar to buying airtime for calls, but this time you are buying access to the internet. When you turn on mobile data, your phone uses the network signal around you to connect you online. That is why if you are in a place with weak network, the internet can be slow or not work well. Your data has a limit, so if you watch many videos or download big files, it can finish quickly.

step2.heading: How to turn mobile data on and off
step2.content: Turning mobile data on and off helps you control how much you spend. One easy way is to pull down the notification bar at the top of your screen by swiping down. Look for a button that says Data, Mobile data, or shows a small internet icon, then tap it to turn it on or off. Another way is to open Settings, then look for Network & Internet, Connections, or SIM & Mobile network, depending on your phone. Inside there, you will see Mobile data where you can switch it on or off. Practice it a few times so you can quickly save data when you are not using the internet.

step3.heading: What is WiFi and how is it different from mobile data?
step3.content: WiFi is another way to connect to the internet, but it usually comes from a router (a small box) in a home, school, café, or office. Think of mobile data like a private borehole you pay for—when you buy it, you can fetch water (internet) anywhere you have network. WiFi is like a public tap that works only when you are near it, and it may be free or shared by a place. WiFi often feels faster and cheaper because you are not paying for each small use like mobile data. But WiFi is not everywhere, so when you leave that place, it stops working. Both connect you to the same internet; they are just different roads to reach it.

step4.heading: How to connect to a WiFi network
step4.content: First, open Settings on your phone and look for WiFi or Network & Internet. Tap WiFi and you will see a list of available WiFi names (these names are called networks). Choose the WiFi you want, like your house WiFi or a school WiFi, then tap it. If it asks for a password, type the password carefully and tap Connect or Join. When it connects, you will see a message like Connected and the WiFi icon will appear at the top of your screen. If the password is wrong, do not worry—check the password again and try slowly.

step5.heading: How to know if you are connected to the internet
step5.content: Look at the top of your phone screen where small icons appear. If you are using WiFi, you will see the WiFi icon that looks like small curved lines. If you are using mobile data, you may see 3G, 4G, LTE, or 5G near your network bars—those letters show the type of mobile internet connection. If you see network bars but no 3G/4G sign and data is off, you may not be connected to the internet. Another simple test is to open a website or WhatsApp and see if messages can send. If nothing is loading, your data may be off, finished, or the network is weak.

step6.heading: Saving your data and using it wisely
step6.content: Data is money, so it is smart to use it wisely. Turn off mobile data when you are not using the internet, especially when you are sleeping or walking around. Use WiFi when it is available, because it usually saves your mobile data. Be careful with apps that play videos automatically, because they can finish your data quickly without you noticing. You can also restrict background data in Settings so apps do not use data when you are not actively using them. Finally, if you want to download big files, do it when you are on WiFi so your mobile data does not disappear fast.

step1.heading: What is a web browser?
step1.content: A web browser is an app on your phone that helps you visit websites. Think of it like a door that opens into the internet, so you can enter different places online. Popular browsers include Chrome, Firefox, and Opera Mini, and they all do the same job in slightly different ways. When you want to read news, search for information, or open a website like Google, you are using a browser. If you hear the word browser, just remember: it is the app that helps you move around the internet.

step2.heading: How to open a browser and find the address bar
step2.content: First, look on your home screen for Chrome, Opera Mini, or any browser icon, then tap it to open. When the browser opens, look near the top of the screen for a long box where you can type. That long box is called the address bar, and it is where you type a website address or even type a search. On some browsers, the address bar can also be in the middle of the screen at first, then move to the top after you start browsing. Tap inside the address bar and your keyboard will show up. Now you are ready to type a website address or a question.

step3.heading: What is a website address (URL) and how does it work?
step3.content: A website address is also called a URL, which simply means the link that tells the internet where to go. Think of it like a house address in your area—if you don’t have the correct address, you may enter the wrong house. Examples of website addresses are www.google.com or www.bbc.com/hausa. When you type a URL in the address bar and press Go, the browser uses the internet to find that exact website. Small mistakes like adding extra letters can take you to a fake or wrong website. That is why it is good to type slowly and check the spelling.

step4.heading: How to navigate a website
step4.content: When a website opens, you can scroll by swiping up and down to read. If you see blue text or underlined words, those are often links—tap them to open another page. If you go somewhere you don’t want, tap the back button to return to the previous page. If a page is not loading well, you can refresh it by tapping the refresh icon (it looks like a small circle arrow). Take your time and tap carefully, because many websites have many buttons and adverts. With practice, moving around websites will start to feel normal.

step5.heading: Bookmarking a website you want to visit again
step5.content: A bookmark is like saving a place so you can return easily, like saving a contact number instead of memorizing it. When you are on a website you like, open the browser menu (three dots) or look for a star icon. Tap the star or choose Add to bookmarks, and the browser will save that website for you. Next time, you can open your bookmarks and tap the website name instead of typing it again. This is helpful for websites you use often, like news pages or school resources. It also reduces mistakes, because you won’t be retyping the address every time.

step6.heading: Browsing safely and closing tabs
step6.content: Tabs are like multiple pages open at the same time in your browser, and too many tabs can make your phone slow. When you finish reading something, close the tab so your phone works smoothly. Be careful about saving passwords on a phone that other people use, because someone else can enter your accounts. If you use someone’s phone, it is wise to log out of your account after use and clear browsing history if needed. Clearing history means removing the list of websites you visited from that phone. Most importantly, if a page looks suspicious or shows strange pop-ups, close it immediately.

step1.heading: What is a search engine and how does it work?
step1.content: A search engine is a tool that helps you find information on the internet. Google is the most common one, and you can think of it like a giant library where you can find almost anything if you ask well. It is also like asking a very knowledgeable friend who knows where to look for answers. When you type a question or some keywords, Google searches many websites and shows you results. It does not create the information; it only points you to places where the information is found. That is why you still need to choose carefully which result to trust.

step2.heading: How to type a good search query
step2.content: A search query simply means what you type into Google to search. You can type a full sentence, but keywords often work faster and give clearer results. For example, instead of typing “I want to find past questions for WAEC exams,” you can type “WAEC past questions” or “WAEC English past questions PDF.” If you want a specific place, add the location, like “best suya in Kaduna” or “JAMB office Kano address.” Keep your words simple and focused on what you need. With time, you will learn which words bring the best results.

step3.heading: Understanding search results
step3.content: When you search on Google, you will see a list of results on a page. Each result has a title (blue text), a small description, and a link to a website. The description gives you a small idea of what the page contains before you open it. The first result is not always the best or most trusted; sometimes it is just the most popular or even an advert. Some results may say Sponsored or Ad, which means someone paid for it to appear. Take a moment to read the title and description so you choose wisely.

step4.heading: How to know if a search result is trustworthy
step4.content: Trustworthy websites often have a clear name and are known for accurate information. Government websites usually end with .gov, like jamb.gov.ng, and they are often reliable for official updates. Educational websites sometimes end with .edu, and they can be useful for learning, but still read carefully. News organizations like BBC and reputable Nigerian news sites are also more reliable than random blogs. If a website has too many ads, confusing writing, or big shocking headlines, be careful. When in doubt, compare the same information on another trusted site before believing it.

step5.heading: Searching safely and avoiding dangerous content
step5.content: Not every link you see is safe, even if it appears on Google. Some websites are fake and may try to collect your personal information like passwords, bank details, or phone numbers. Before you click, look at the website address and check if it looks normal and correctly spelled. Avoid pages that immediately ask you to “log in” or “enter your details” without a good reason. If a site shows scary pop-ups like “your phone is infected,” close it—those are often tricks. Searching safely means moving slowly, checking addresses, and not giving out private information.

step6.heading: Using Google for everyday Nigerian life
step6.content: Google can help you in simple everyday ways. You can search for “MTN data plan 1GB price” or “Airtel night plan” to understand current offers. You can search for the nearest hospital, pharmacy, or police station by typing your location. Students can search for “JAMB result checker” or “NECO timetable” to get official updates, but make sure you use the correct website. You can also search for recipes like “jollof rice recipe” or “miyan kuka” and follow step-by-step instructions. The more you use search for real needs, the more confident you become online.

step1.heading: What makes a website trustworthy?
step1.content: Not every website on the internet is honest or safe, just like not every person you meet in a busy market is trustworthy. A trustworthy website usually has clear information, a real purpose, and a name that people recognize. It does not try to rush you with fear or sweet promises like “free money now.” It also does not hide important details or force you to click many strange pop-ups. Many trusted websites are connected to known organizations like government, schools, banks, or reputable news companies. Learning to recognize trustworthy websites will save you from scams and confusion.

step2.heading: What is HTTPS and the padlock symbol?
step2.content: When a website starts with https, it means the connection is more secure. You may also see a small padlock symbol in the browser bar, and that padlock is a good sign. Think of it like a sealed envelope—your information is protected while traveling between your phone and the website. It does not mean the website is perfect, but it is safer than a site without https. If you are entering sensitive information like passwords, always check for https and the padlock. If you don’t see it, be extra careful or avoid entering personal details.

step3.heading: Recognizing fake or dangerous websites
step3.content: Fake websites often have strange addresses with many numbers, random letters, or misspelled brand names. Some will look unprofessional, with poor writing and many errors, or they may copy the design of a real website but the address is different. Be careful of pop-ups that say you have won a prize, your phone is infected, or you must act immediately. These messages are often traps to make you click or give information. If a website keeps redirecting you to different pages, close it. Your safety is more important than curiosity.

step4.heading: Trusted websites Nigerians use every day
step4.content: Some websites are well known and trusted because they belong to official bodies or reputable organizations. For JAMB information, jamb.gov.ng is trusted because it is an official government website. For NECO updates, neco.gov.ng is trusted for the same reason. For news in Hausa, www.bbc.com/hausa is trusted because BBC is a global news organization with professional reporting. Google.com is trusted as a search engine that helps you find information, but you still need to judge the results you click. Wikipedia.org can be useful for learning, but it is best for general understanding and you should confirm important facts from other sources too.

step5.heading: What to do if a website feels suspicious
step5.content: If a website feels suspicious, the best action is to close the tab immediately. Do not enter your phone number, password, bank details, or any private information on that page. Do not click pop-ups that ask you to install something or claim you won a prize. If you are unsure, ask a trusted friend, older sibling, or someone who understands internet safety. You can also search the website name on Google with the word “scam” to see what others say about it. Staying safe is about being calm, careful, and willing to walk away.

step6.heading: Building your own list of trusted websites
step6.content: A smart habit is to build your own small list of websites you trust. When you find a website that is safe and useful, bookmark it so you can return without searching again. Always double-check the website address before typing personal details, especially on login pages. If you visit a bank or school website, make sure the address is correct every time. Over time, you will know the trusted sites you use for news, learning, and official services. This makes your internet life easier, faster, and much safer.

step1.heading: Email is digital post
step1.content: Email lets you send messages and files to people over the internet.

step2.heading: Every email has an address
step2.content: An address like name@gmail.com helps people send messages to you.

step3.heading: Email is useful for school and work
step3.content: You can use email for forms, jobs, and learning opportunities.

step1.heading: Open Gmail sign up
step1.content: Go to Gmail create account and fill your basic details.

step2.heading: Create a strong password
step2.content: Use letters, numbers, and symbols. Do not use your name only.

step3.heading: Add recovery options
step3.content: Add a phone number so you can recover your account if needed.

step1.heading: Compose a new email
step1.content: Tap compose, add recipient address, then write a clear subject and message.

step2.heading: Read inbox messages
step2.content: Open your inbox regularly to read replies and important updates.

step3.heading: Reply politely
step3.content: Use respectful and short replies so communication stays clear.

step1.heading: Set up your profile
step1.content: Add your name and a simple photo so friends can identify you.

step2.heading: Send text and voice notes
step2.content: Use text for quick messages and voice notes when typing is hard.

step3.heading: Manage group chats
step3.content: Mute noisy groups and avoid forwarding unverified messages.

step1.heading: Protect private chats
step1.content: Do not share personal photos, codes, or sensitive details with strangers.

step2.heading: Spot fake links
step2.content: Ignore links promising free money, gifts, or urgent account fixes.

step3.heading: Block and report abuse
step3.content: Use block and report tools if someone threatens or scams you.

step1.heading: Know what is private
step1.content: Phone number, ID details, and bank information should be kept private.

step2.heading: Share less online
step2.content: Only share what is needed. Too much personal posting can be risky.

step3.heading: Use privacy settings
step3.content: Limit who can see your profile, posts, and status updates.

step1.heading: Watch for urgent pressure
step1.content: Scammers push you to act fast. Slow down and verify first.

step2.heading: Check the sender
step2.content: Look closely at numbers and email addresses before trusting messages.

step3.heading: Do not share OTP codes
step3.content: One-time password codes are private. No real support team will ask for them.

step1.heading: Use long passwords
step1.content: Use at least 10 characters with mixed letters, numbers, and symbols.

step2.heading: Use different passwords
step2.content: Do not use one password for every app. If one leaks, others stay safe.

step3.heading: Store passwords safely
step3.content: Use a trusted password manager or a private notebook kept secure.

step1.heading: Think before posting
step1.content: Ask if this post is safe and respectful before sharing publicly.

step2.heading: Control who contacts you
step2.content: Use privacy controls to reduce unknown messages and harassment.

step3.heading: Avoid harmful challenges
step3.content: Do not join dangerous online trends just because others are doing them.

lesson5.title: What to do if something goes wrong

step1.heading: Act quickly
step1.content: Change password immediately if you think your account was accessed.

step2.heading: Ask for trusted help
step2.content: Contact a trusted friend, family member, or official support channel.

step3.heading: Report and recover
step3.content: Use report tools in the app and follow recovery steps to secure your account.
